lwe 14,2OM
William D. Esselstein
Meyers Nave Riback Silver
& Wilson
401 Mendocino Avenug Suite 100
Santa Rosa, CA 95401
Re:
Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our File No. I-03-293
Dear Mr. Esselstein:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf ofthe five members
of the Menlo Park Fire Protection District Bomd ofDirectors ('Board-),I regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the *Acf)-' Since your
request does not identifu a specific decision3 orproceeding and only seeks our general
guidancg we are treating your request as one for infonnal assistance-a This letter should
not be construed as assistance on any decisions of the Board which may have already
taken place. Our assistance is based on the facts you pres€nt- The FairPolitical Practices
Commission ("Commission') does not act as a finder of fact whem it provides assistance.
(In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
I Although your request was submitted on behalf of all five members ofth Boar4 we arg
responding only on behalf of Directors Sperer and Carpenter, the tro officials uihoseparticipation is in
question- We have no indication that these two officials authorized thc remaining ncmbers ofthe Board to
request advice on their behalf Pursuant to regulation 1S329(bX8XB) D.ritteo adyice or assistree is not
provided jn response to unauthorized third party requests.
' Gover:nment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18-997, ofthe California Code of Regulations.
3 Although your request also specifically asked for advice as to whether Director Sperrer had a
conflict of interest in March 2001, disqualifuing him from participating in a decision of the Board to
appoint Mr. Carpenter to fill a vacant directorship on the Board we decline to offer advice on this decision
as itpertains to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8XA).)
' Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
nt:i]


File No. l-03-293
Page No. 2
QUESTTONS
1. In light of the real estate relationship between Directors Carpenter and
Spencer, would the conflict-of-interest provisions ofthe Actprohibit either one from
voting with the Board on the candidacy of the other to fill a Bomd office?
2. In light of the real estate relationship between Directors Carpenter and
Spencer, would the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit either one from
voting with the Board on whether to allow the other to be reimbmsed for travel expenses
to attend, in an official capacity, a meeting or conference?
CONCLUSIONS
1. No. A conflict of interest is based upon financial interests. Since a Board
officer serves without compensation, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to
elect an individual to a Board office will have a material financial effect upon the
official's personal finances or any other of the official's economic interest.
2. Yes. The relationship between the two directors in their coillmon ownership of
the residential real estate parcel is that of a lender and borrower. Therefore, each director
is a source of income and economic interest to the other. Sinoe anyreasonably
foreseeable financial effect at all upon an indirridual who is a source of income to a public
official who is involved in the decision is deemed material underthe Comrnission's
regulations, Mr. Carpenter and Mr. Spencerhave a conflict of interest which, in the
absence of an exception, disqualifies them from voting on expenscreimbrrs€,ments to be
provided to the other.
FACTS5
The Menlo Park Fire Protection Diskict Board of Directors is a five-member body
whose members serve without compensation. The Board elects officers from within its
membership and directors serye as officers without 
on- Members, in either
their capacity as directors or officers of the Board, are entitled to seek reimbursement for
expenses incurred while on Board business. In addition, mernbers receive a nominal per
diem allotrnent in connection with attending Board meetings. BothMr. Carpenter and
Mr. Spencer are members of the Board.
a
In April of 2000, Mr. Spemcer enterd into an agreernerrt with h[r. Carpemter and
his wife, acting through their family kust, to jointly.purchase propertlr which
Mr. Spencer would then use as his principal residence. Pursuant to this agreerrent,
Mr. Spencer and the Carpenter Family Trust own this property as tenants in @rnmon,
with the greater (90%) ownership interest held by the trust. Mr. Spencer pays rent of
more than $500 annually to the Carpenter Family Trust, plus an additional amormt in
5 The facts below are those provided in your initial request and in subsequent telephone
conversations held with Commission staffon December 19,2003, and January 8,2}M,and your letter
dated December 29, 2003.


File No. I-03-293
Page No. 3
order to acquire, over time, the trust's ownership interest in this property. At present, the
respective ownership interests of the trust and Mr. Spencer are valued at more than
$2,000. You state that this common ownership in the real property is likely to continue
into the foreseeable future.
The Carpenter Family Trust is a revocable trust for which Director Carpenter and
his wife are both sole trustees and sole beneficiaries-
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making participating in making or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest
(regulation l8700OXl) - (8)). This standard analysis is discussed below.
l. & 2. Are Mr. Spencer and Mr. Carpenter public oflicials making participating
in making or influencing a goverarmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibitions apply only to public officials. As membef,s
of the Menlo Park Fire Protection District's Bomd ofDirectors, Mr. Spencerand Mr.
Carpenter me public offi cials. (Section 82048; regulation I 870 I (a).)
As directors, unless disqualified under the conflict-of-interest provisions, they
will make, participate in making, or use their official positions to influence governmental
decisions of the Board, including voting on candidates to fill openings for Board offices
or decisiolts regarding the reimbursement of travel-related expenses to fellow board
members^6
3. What are these officials'economic interests?
The economic interests which might giverise to a conflict of interes! implicatd
by your facts, are found at section 87103 and are defined in regulations 18703-18703.5.
The only economic interests you have described are those arising from the joint
ownership of the residential property.
A. Sources of Income - A public official has an economic interest in a*y souroe
of incomg including promised incomg which aggrcgates to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision at issue. (Section 87103(c); regulation 13703.3.) Incomg
6 If a public official's offce is listed in section 87200 (-872Dfilers" irclude members of a
county's board ofsupervisors) and he or she has a conflict ofinterest in a decision noticed at a public
meeting, then he or she must: (l) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identifu each
type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details ofthe economic interest, as discussed
in regulation 18702.5(bXl[B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the
room for the duration of the discussion and,/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, corsent calendars,
absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in
regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)


File No. I-03-293
Page No. 4
for this pu{pose, "includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in
which the indiyidual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly orbeneficially, a
l0-percent or greater interest." (Section 82030(a)-)
Mr-
As a tenant, Mr. Spencer provides the CarpenterFamily Trust with income which
aggregates to $500 or more over any glvelr l2-month period. The question is whether
this income to the trust is also to be considered as income to Mr. Carpenter, so that Mr.
Spencer is a source of income to, and numbers irmong the economic interests of, Mr.
Carpenter. The answer to this question depends on whether, under these facts, it is
appropriate to "piercd'through the organizational structure ofthe trust.
Business tnrsts are business entities under the Act. ($ S20O5; Reg 18705.1(c)(a).)
However, a family trust is tlpically considered a nonprofit entity. (Auran Advice Letter,
No. A-01-138:' Hunter Advice Letter, No. I-92-594.)' ln certain instances, the
organizational structure of a nonprofit entity will be'lierced" and income to the entity
will regarded as income to one or more members ofthe emtity who exercise actual control
over the entity's decisions- (Fanell Advice Letter, No.l-O2-227; Lucas Advice Letter,
No. 4-96-248.) ln this instancg the Carpenter FamilyTrust is arevocable trust and Mr.
Carpenter and his wife are both the sole trustees and the sole beneficiaries of this trust. In
these circumstances, for pu{poses of the conflict-of-int€rest provisions of the Act it is
appropriate to '!ierce" through the trust entity and attribute the income received by the
trust as income received by Mr. Carpenter and his wife.
Mr. Carpenter individually has a 5Uo/obeneficial ownership interest in this trust
and also has a community property share in his spouse's 50% beneficial ownership
interest in this trust. Thus, a source of income to the trust aggregating $6678 or more
over the 12-month period preceding a desision ofthe Boarrd will be considered a sotrce
of income to Mr. Carpenter and nurnber among his economic interests. We assume that
Mr. Spencer provides the trust with this amount ormore in paynent for the rental of his
pimary residencg over any gven l2-month period.e Thus,IvIr. Spencer is an economic
interest to Mr. Carpenter.
' The principal distinction between a business trust and an ordinary trust is formd in the purpose.
The purpose ofa business trust is to carry/ on a business enterprise forprofit whereas the purpose ofthe
ordinary trust is to conserve the property and other assets ofthe bus! with incid€otal powtrs ofsale and
investment. (Barisone Advice Irtter, No. A44-073;M-77436; Hunter Advice Irtter, above.)
8 Mr. Carpenter's combinedTl%pro rata share of tust income rleans that income to the trust of
$667 yielJts income to him equaling the $500 threshold applicable to sources ofincome to him.
e This same analysis holds true for Mr. Spencer's pa)men8, above and beyond rent, to "buy out''
the Carpenter Family Trust's ownership interest in this property. Thus, Mr. Sperrer would be a source of
income and economic interest to Mr. Carpenter on the basis of thesepa)ments, as well as his rental
payments.


File No. I-03-293
Page No. 5
Mr. Spencer
The business relationship between Mr. Carpenter and Mr. Spencer is best viewed
as a nontraditional means.of financing Mr. Spencer's purchase ofhis principal residence.
In essence, Mr. carpenterlo is "lending" the purch^" pri"" for this p.opoty, secured
through the trust's 90% ownership interest. Mr. Spencer's "buyout" ofthis ownership
interest ove,r a period of years has the same effect as would a monthly mortgage payment
inasmuch as his equity interest in this property increases over time until he ultimately
acquires sole title. Functionally, Mr. Carpenter occupies the same position in this
transaction as would a private mortgage lender.
A loan is considered under the Act to be'tncome" to the loan's recipient (section
82030(a) unless any of the exceptions under section 82030(b) apply. These exceptions
include:
"Any loan or loans from a commercial lending institution which are made
in the lender's regular course of business on terms available to members
to the public without regard to official status." (Srction S2030(bXS).)
and;
"Any indebtedness created as part of a retail installment or credit card
transaction if made in the lender's regular @urse ofbusiness on terms
available to members of thepublic without regard to official status.,,
(Section 82030(bX1 0).)
Neither of these exceptions under section 820300) apply in these circunstances.
Thus, through this loan Mr. Carpenter is a source of income which is an economic
interest to Mr. Spencer.
B. Real Property -- A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2). An indirect investnent orinterest in real propertymeans, among
other things, any real property owned by a business entity in which the official olvns
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.)
Mr. Carpenter
Mr. Carpenter has an indirect interest in the jointly-owned real propertyby virtue
of his 50% beneficial interest in the Carpenter Family Trust- Since this tnrst has a9o%o
ownership interest in the property, we assume that the value ofthis ownership interest is
$2,000 or more. This real property is included among Mr. carpenter's economic
interests.
t0 For the reasorx stated previously, we are disregarding the intermediary of the family trust and
are attributing its actions to Mr. Carpenter and his spouse.


File No. I-03-293
Page No. 6
Mr. Spencer
Mr. Spencer has a l0Yo ownership interest in this real property which seryes as
his primary residence- Given the nature of this property, we assume that the value of his
ownership interest is $2,000 or more. This real property is included among Mr.
Spencer's economic interests.
4. WiII these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A. Real Property - 
The appropriate standards for determining direct or indirect
involvement of an official's economic interest in real property are found in regulation
18704.2. Under this regulation, real property is considered directly involved in a
governmental decision if either: "l) [t]he real property in which the official has an
interest, or any part of that real propert5r, is located within 500 feet ofthe boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of theproperty which is the subject of the governmental
decision..." or the governmental decision is of a type which falls into any of the five
categories listed in regulation |87O .2@)Q) - (aX6). If a real property interest is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considerd to be indirectly involved for
the purpose of determining the appropriate materiality standard. (Regulation
18704.2@)(2).)
In this instancg the jointly-owned real property does not meet any of the criteria
under the regulations to be considered directly involved in decisions regarding election to
Board office or reimburse,rnent of travel-related exlrenses. Mr. Spencer's and Mr.
Carpenter's jointly-owned real property will be considered indirectly involved in these
decisions.
B. Sources of Income - Anyperson, including abusiness entity, which is a
source of income in which a public official has an economic interest, is directly involved
in a governmental decision if that person, either directly or by an agent initiates a
proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar reques! or is a named party
iru or is the subject of a proceeding before the official or the official's agency.
(Regulation 18704.1(a)(l)-(a)(2).) A business entity or souroe of inoome is the subject of
aproceeding concerning a decision before the official orthe official's agency ifthe
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, orrevocation of any license,
permit, or other entitleurent to, or contract with, the source of income. @egulation
t87M.t(a)(2).)
1. Election to Board Office -- Should either Mr. Spenoer or Mr. Carpenter be
nominated for election to a Board office, as a candidate for election theywould be a
"named party in, or the subject of, a proceding' and considcred to be directly involved
in the vote on their election to that office.
2. Reimbursement of Travel-Related Expenses - Presumably, under the Board's
procedures, reimbursement to directors of travel-related expenses would only be at issue
once a claim for reimbursernent has been filed. Therefore, should Mr. Spencer or Mr.


File No. l-03-293
Page No. 7
Carpenter seek reimbursement of travel-related expenses, they each would be directly
involved in the Board's decision whether to grant such reimbursernent on their respective
claims-
5. & 6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably foreseeable
that the linancial effects of the governmental decision upon these oflicial's economic
interest will meet this materiality standard?
A. Materiality Standards
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a govemmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different standmds apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the agency's decision.
Real Property: Under the Commission's regulations, it is presurned that a
governmental decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
upon real property which is indirectly involved in that decision. (Regulations
18705.20)(1) and OX2).) This presumption may be rebutted under specific
circumstances described in the commission's regulations. (Regulations
I 8705.2(bX I XA)-(c), and I 8705.2O)(2XA)-(E).)
Sources of Income: Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect, even one penny,
on aperson who is a source of income to apublic official and who is directlyinvolved in
a decision before the official's agencS is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)
B. Reasonably Foreseeable
An effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. @egulation 1S706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foresoeablg but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) When there exists an "extra
degree of remoteness" betw@n a particular goveimmental decision and a financial effect
upon an official's economic interests, the Commission has opined that the financial effect
is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner.)
C. Applied
1. Election to Bomd Office: Election of a directorto a Board office does not
entitle the director/officer to a salary for serving as a Bomd officer. While a director or
officer may be entitled to receive a per diem for attending Board meetings and may be
eligible for reimbursement of travel-related expenses, mere election to office is not
tantamount to receiving these financial benefits. Upon election, these financial benefits
are a "mere possibility'' and their receipt is subject to intervening events, including


File No. I-03-293
Page No. 8
subsequent Board decisions. In addition, since both Mr. Spencer and Mr. Carpenter are
already directors, should either or both be elected to a Board office, the per diem they
receive for attending Board meetings would be the same as if the Board office were not
assumed and they attended solely in their capaaty as directors.
It appears, therefore, that election to Board office would not have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon the candidate- Neither Mr. Spencer nor Mr.
Carpenter would have a conflict of interest prohibiting either from voting on the
candidacy of the other for a Board office. Nevertheless, ultimately this is a facfual
question and as such, is to be decided bythepublic official and not us.
2. Reimbursement of Travel-Related Expenses: If a directorvotes upon the
expense reimbursement to be provided to another director who is also a source of income
to him, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a financial effect upon the
potential recipient. Since any financial effect at all is deeined material rmder the
applicable regulation, above, both Mr. Spencer and Mr. Carpenter will have a conflict of
interest which, in the absence of an exception, will disqualiffthem from voting on an
expense reimbursement claim submitted bythe other.
7. & 8. '5Public Generally' and (Legally Required Participation, Exceptions
. Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic int€rest is not distinguishable from the
effect on thepublic generally. (Section &3|L};regulation 18707.) Step eight is an
exception that applies when the official's participation in a governmental decision is
legally required. (Section 87101 ; regulation 18703.) Both of these narrowly conskued
exceptions are fact-driven and you provide no facts indicating that these exceptions apply
here.
If you have any other questions regarding this mattetr, please contact me at (er6)
322-5660.
Enclosure

Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca

Glick
Counsel, Legal Division

provided to the other.

include:
"Any loan or loans from a commercial lending institution which are made
in the lender's regular course of business on terms available to members
to the public without regard to official status." (Srction S2030(bXS).)
and;
"Any indebtedness created as part of a retail installment or credit card
transaction if made in the lender's regular @urse ofbusiness on terms
available to members of thepublic without regard to official status.,,
(Section 82030(bX1 0).)
Neither of these exceptions under section 820300) apply in these circunstances.
Thus, through this loan Mr. Carpenter is a source of income which is an economic
interest to Mr. Spencer.
B. Real Property -- A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2). An indirect investnent orinterest in real propertymeans, among
other things, any real property owned by a business entity in which the official olvns
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.)
Mr. Carpenter
Mr. Carpenter has an indirect interest in the jointly-owned real propertyby virtue
of his 50% beneficial interest in the Carpenter Family Trust- Since this tnrst has a9o%o
ownership interest in the property, we assume that the value ofthis ownership interest is
$2,000 or more. This real property is included among Mr. carpenter's economic
interests.
t0 For the reasorx stated previously, we are disregarding the intermediary of the family trust and
are attributing its actions to Mr. Carpenter and his spouse.
Mr. Spencer
Mr. Spencer has a l0Yo ownership interest in this real property which seryes as
his primary residence- Given the nature of this property, we assume that the value of his
ownership interest is $2,000 or more. This real property is included among Mr.
Spencer's economic interests.
4. WiII these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A. Real Property - 
The appropriate standards for determining direct or indirect
involvement of an official's economic interest in real property are found in regulation
18704.2. Under this regulation, real property is considered directly involved in a
governmental decision if either: "l) [t]he real property in which the official has an
interest, or any part of that real propert5r, is located within 500 feet ofthe boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of theproperty which is the subject of the governmental
decision..." or the governmental decision is of a type which falls into any of the five
categories listed in regulation |87O .2@)Q) - (aX6). If a real property interest is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considerd to be indirectly involved for
the purpose of determining the appropriate materiality standard. (Regulation
18704.2@)(2).)
In this instancg the jointly-owned real property does not meet any of the criteria
under the regulations to be considered directly involved in decisions regarding election to
Board office or reimburse,rnent of travel-related exlrenses. Mr. Spencer's and Mr.
Carpenter's jointly-owned real property will be considered indirectly involved in these
decisions.
B. Sources of Income - Anyperson, including abusiness entity, which is a
source of income in which a public official has an economic interest, is directly involved
in a governmental decision if that person, either directly or by an agent initiates a
proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar reques! or is a named party
iru or is the subject of a proceeding before the official or the official's agency.
(Regulation 18704.1(a)(l)-(a)(2).) A business entity or souroe of inoome is the subject of
aproceeding concerning a decision before the official orthe official's agency ifthe
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, orrevocation of any license,
permit, or other entitleurent to, or contract with, the source of income. @egulation
t87M.t(a)(2).)
1. Election to Board Office -- Should either Mr. Spenoer or Mr. Carpenter be
nominated for election to a Board office, as a candidate for election theywould be a
"named party in, or the subject of, a proceding' and considcred to be directly involved
in the vote on their election to that office.
2. Reimbursement of Travel-Related Expenses - Presumably, under the Board's
procedures, reimbursement to directors of travel-related expenses would only be at issue
once a claim for reimbursernent has been filed. Therefore, should Mr. Spencer or Mr.


File No. l-03-293
Carpenter seek reimbursement of travel-related expenses, they each would be directly
involved in the Board's decision whether to grant such reimbursernent on their respective
claims-
5. & 6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably foreseeable
that the linancial effects of the governmental decision upon these oflicial's economic
interest will meet this materiality standard?
A. Materiality Standards
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a govemmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different standmds apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the agency's decision.
Real Property: Under the Commission's regulations, it is presurned that a
governmental decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
upon real property which is indirectly involved in that decision. (Regulations
18705.20)(1) and OX2).) This presumption may be rebutted under specific
circumstances described in the commission's regulations. (Regulations
I 8705.2(bX I XA)-(c), and I 8705.2O)(2XA)-(E).)
Sources of Income: Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect, even one penny,
on aperson who is a source of income to apublic official and who is directlyinvolved in
a decision before the official's agencS is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)
B. Reasonably Foreseeable
An effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. @egulation 1S706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foresoeablg but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) When there exists an "extra
degree of remoteness" betw@n a particular goveimmental decision and a financial effect
upon an official's economic interests, the Commission has opined that the financial effect
is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner.)
C. Applied
1. Election to Bomd Office: Election of a directorto a Board office does not
entitle the director/officer to a salary for serving as a Bomd officer. While a director or
officer may be entitled to receive a per diem for attending Board meetings and may be
eligible for reimbursement of travel-related expenses, mere election to office is not
tantamount to receiving these financial benefits. Upon election, these financial benefits
are a "mere possibility'' and their receipt is subject to intervening events, including
subsequent Board decisions. In addition, since both Mr. Spencer and Mr. Carpenter are
already directors, should either or both be elected to a Board office, the per diem they
receive for attending Board meetings would be the same as if the Board office were not
assumed and they attended solely in their capaaty as directors.
It appears, therefore, that election to Board office would not have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon the candidate- Neither Mr. Spencer nor Mr.
Carpenter would have a conflict of interest prohibiting either from voting on the
candidacy of the other for a Board office. Nevertheless, ultimately this is a facfual
question and as such, is to be decided bythepublic official and not us.
2. Reimbursement of Travel-Related Expenses: If a directorvotes upon the
expense reimbursement to be provided to another director who is also a source of income
to him, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a financial effect upon the
potential recipient. Since any financial effect at all is deeined material rmder the
applicable regulation, above, both Mr. Spencer and Mr. Carpenter will have a conflict of
interest which, in the absence of an exception, will disqualiffthem from voting on an
expense reimbursement claim submitted bythe other.
7. & 8. '5Public Generally' and (Legally Required Participation, Exceptions
. Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic int€rest is not distinguishable from the
effect on thepublic generally. (Section &3|L};regulation 18707.) Step eight is an
exception that applies when the official's participation in a governmental decision is
legally required. (Section 87101 ; regulation 18703.) Both of these narrowly conskued
exceptions are fact-driven and you provide no facts indicating that these exceptions apply
here.

QUESTTONS
1. In light of the real estate relationship between Directors Carpenter and
Spencer, would the conflict-of-interest provisions ofthe Actprohibit either one from
voting with the Board on the candidacy of the other to fill a Bomd office?
2. In light of the real estate relationship between Directors Carpenter and
Spencer, would the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit either one from
voting with the Board on whether to allow the other to be reimbmsed for travel expenses
to attend, in an official capacity, a meeting or conference?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. A conflict of interest is based upon financial interests. Since a Board
officer serves without compensation, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to
elect an individual to a Board office will have a material financial effect upon the
official's personal finances or any other of the official's economic interest.
2. Yes. The relationship between the two directors in their coillmon ownership of
the residential real estate parcel is that of a lender and borrower. Therefore, each director
is a source of income and economic interest to the other. Sinoe anyreasonably
foreseeable financial effect at all upon an indirridual who is a source of income to a public
official who is involved in the decision is deemed material underthe Comrnission's
regulations, Mr. Carpenter and Mr. Spencerhave a conflict of interest which, in the
absence of an exception, disqualifies them from voting on expenscreimbrrs€,ments to be
provided to the other.
FACTS5
The Menlo Park Fire Protection Diskict Board of Directors is a five-member body
whose members serve without compensation. The Board elects officers from within its
membership and directors serye as officers without 
on- Members, in either
their capacity as directors or officers of the Board, are entitled to seek reimbursement for
expenses incurred while on Board business. In addition, mernbers receive a nominal per
diem allotrnent in connection with attending Board meetings. BothMr. Carpenter and
Mr. Spencer are members of the Board.
a
In April of 2000, Mr. Spemcer enterd into an agreernerrt with h[r. Carpemter and
his wife, acting through their family kust, to jointly.purchase propertlr which
Mr. Spencer would then use as his principal residence. Pursuant to this agreerrent,
Mr. Spencer and the Carpenter Family Trust own this property as tenants in @rnmon,
with the greater (90%) ownership interest held by the trust. Mr. Spencer pays rent of
more than $500 annually to the Carpenter Family Trust, plus an additional amormt in

order to acquire, over time, the trust's ownership interest in this property. At present, the
respective ownership interests of the trust and Mr. Spencer are valued at more than
$2,000. You state that this common ownership in the real property is likely to continue
into the foreseeable future.
The Carpenter Family Trust is a revocable trust for which Director Carpenter and
his wife are both sole trustees and sole beneficiaries-
